T8D06.explanation: PSK stands for Phase Shift Keying. It is a digital modulation method where the phase of the carrier is changed to represent data.
why_wrong: Pulse Shift Keying is not the meaning of PSK in amateur digital-mode terminology.
Packet Short Keying is not a real expansion here.
Phased Slide Keying is just a distractor phrase.

T8D07.hint: DMR gets efficient repeater use by fitting two digital voice paths into one 12.5 kHz channel using time slots.
T8D07.explanation: DMR is a technique for time-multiplexing two digital voice signals on a single 12.5 kHz repeater channel. In simple terms, two conversations share the same channel by taking turns rapidly in time.
T8D07.mnemonic: DMR means two time slots sharing one channel.
why_wrong: That describes a tracking idea, not DMR.
DMR is not an automatic vehicle logging method.
DMR does not work by transmitting on two repeater inputs simultaneously.

T8D08.hint: Packet radio frames include addressing information, error checking, and ways to recover from errors. Which answer includes all of that?
T8D08.explanation: Packet radio transmissions include a checksum for error detection, a header with destination information, and automatic repeat request behavior in case of errors. Because all are part of packet communication concepts, 'All these choices are correct' is right.
T8D08.mnemonic: Packets need addressing, error checks, and retries.
why_wrong: A checksum is included, but it is only one piece of the whole packet system.
Headers matter too, but not by themselves enough for the complete answer.
Automatic repeat request is also part of the picture, but still only part.

T8D09.hint: CW is the exam pool shorthand for continuous-wave Morse operation. What does it refer to in practical ham use?
T8D09.explanation: CW is another name for a Morse code transmission. In amateur-radio operating language, saying you are on CW means you are using Morse code.
T8D09.mnemonic: CW on the exam means Morse code operation.
why_wrong: CW here is not describing a propagation type.
It is not the specific 2-meter FM digital mode suggested here.
Coil winding has nothing to do with the operating meaning of CW.

T8D10.hint: WSJT-X was designed for weak-signal digital work, including EME, propagation beacons, and meteor scatter. Which answer captures that full range?
T8D10.explanation: WSJT-X software supports Earth-Moon-Earth operation, weak-signal propagation beacons, and meteor scatter work. Because all of those activities are supported, the correct answer is 'All these choices are correct.'
T8D10.mnemonic: WSJT-X covers several weak-signal specialties, not just one.
why_wrong: EME is supported, but it is only one of several WSJT-X uses.
Weak-signal beacon work is also supported, but again not the only one.
Meteor scatter is supported too, but not by itself the complete answer.

T8D11.hint: ARQ systems do not just detect errors; they ask for missing or damaged data to be sent again. Which choice says that clearly?
T8D11.explanation: An ARQ transmission system is an error-correction method in which the receiving station detects errors and sends a request for retransmission. The key idea is automatic retry after an error is noticed.
T8D11.mnemonic: ARQ means errors trigger automatic repeats.
why_wrong: ARQ is not limited to video transmissions.
ARQ is about error recovery, not encryption of satellite commands.
Data compression is a different concept from automatic retransmission.

T8D12.hint: A ham mesh network is basically a data network built from Wi-Fi-style hardware adapted for amateur use. Which choice says that?
T8D12.explanation: An amateur radio mesh network is an amateur-radio-based data network using commercial Wi-Fi equipment with modified firmware. Think of it as repurposed networking gear used to build RF data links under amateur rules.
T8D12.mnemonic: Mesh often means ham networking built from modified Wi-Fi gear.
why_wrong: That describes a digital voice idea, not a mesh data network.
Mesh networking is not a modified satellite-TV system.
Internet repeater linking is a different concept from a radio mesh network.

T8D13.hint: FT8 became popular because it can decode and make contacts even when signals are very weak. Which answer describes that strength?
T8D13.explanation: FT8 is a digital mode capable of low signal-to-noise operation. It is designed so stations can still exchange minimal contact information even when signals are too weak for easy voice communication.
T8D13.mnemonic: FT8 is built for weak-signal decoding.
why_wrong: FT8 is not a wideband FM voice mode.
The '8' in FT8 does not mean eight-channel FM multiplexing.
FT8 is not a slow-scan TV mode.

T9A01.hint: A beam antenna is directional rather than omnidirectional. What is its defining behavior?
T9A01.explanation: A beam antenna concentrates signals in one direction. That directional focus increases performance in the favored direction compared with an antenna that radiates equally all around.
why_wrong: Some beam antennas may use aluminum, but that is not what makes them beam antennas.
A beam is the opposite of omnidirectional in how it concentrates energy.
Phase relationships can exist in antenna design, but reversing phase is not the definition of a beam antenna.

T9A02.hint: Antenna loading is used when the antenna is physically short but you want it to behave electrically longer. How is that commonly done?
T9A02.explanation: A common type of antenna loading is electrically lengthening the antenna by inserting inductors in the radiating elements. Loading coils help a short antenna resonate as if it were longer.
T9A02.mnemonic: Loading usually means adding inductance to make a short antenna act longer.
why_wrong: A resistor wastes power and is not the normal way to make an antenna resonant.
A spring may add flexibility, but that is mechanical, not electrical loading.
Wind-strengthening is a structural change, not antenna loading.

T9A03.hint: Polarization follows the orientation of the antenna's electric field. If a simple dipole is parallel to Earth's surface, what polarization does it have?
T9A03.explanation: A simple dipole oriented parallel to Earth's surface is horizontally polarized. In exam questions, horizontal orientation means horizontal polarization.
why_wrong: Ground-wave describes a propagation path, not an antenna polarization.
Traveling-wave is a different antenna concept and not the key point here.
Vertical polarization would require the antenna to be oriented vertically, not parallel to the ground.

T9A04.hint: The short flexible rubber-duck antenna on a handheld is a compromise. What is the main performance drawback compared with a full quarter-wave antenna?
T9A04.explanation: The short flexible antenna supplied with many handhelds has low efficiency compared with a full-sized quarter-wave antenna. Because it is electrically small and compromised, less of your transmitter power is effectively radiated.
why_wrong: A flexible handheld antenna does not transmit only circular polarization.
They are usually chosen because they are fairly practical and flexible, not because fragility is the main issue.
Since the other statements are not correct, 'all' cannot be right.

T9A05.hint: A shorter antenna resonates at a higher frequency, while a longer one resonates lower. Which change raises the resonant frequency of a dipole?
T9A05.explanation: Shortening a dipole increases its resonant frequency. That is why trimming an antenna is the common adjustment when you need resonance to move upward in frequency.
why_wrong: Lengthening a dipole moves resonance lower, not higher.
Series loading coils electrically lengthen the antenna, which also lowers resonant frequency.
Capacitive end loading usually makes the antenna act electrically longer, not shorter.

T9A06.hint: Among the listed antennas, which one is specifically designed to provide strong directional gain?
T9A06.explanation: A Yagi offers the greatest gain of the listed choices because it is a directional array that focuses energy into a favored direction using multiple elements.
T9A06.mnemonic: When you want gain and direction, think Yagi.
why_wrong: A 5/8-wave vertical can have useful gain, but not like a directional Yagi in this comparison.
An isotropic antenna is only a theoretical reference, not a practical high-gain choice.
A J-pole is practical and simple, but it does not offer the greatest gain among these options.

T9A07.hint: Using a handheld with its little antenna inside a car often works poorly because the vehicle body blocks and absorbs RF. What is the main disadvantage?
T9A07.explanation: Signal strength is reduced due to the shielding effect of the vehicle. The metal body of the vehicle makes it harder for the antenna inside to radiate and receive efficiently.
T9A07.mnemonic: Inside the car, the car shields the signal.
why_wrong: Bandwidth change is not the main issue being tested.
SWR may vary, but the key operating problem is shielding by the vehicle body.
Because the other statements are not the main correct reason, 'all' is not right.

T9A08.hint: A quarter wavelength at 146 MHz is a little under 20 inches. Which answer is closest?
T9A08.explanation: The approximate length of a quarter-wave vertical antenna for 146 MHz is 19 inches. This is a standard rule-of-thumb length for 2-meter antennas.
T9A08.mnemonic: Two meters, quarter wave: about nineteen inches.
why_wrong: 112 inches is far too long for a 146 MHz quarter-wave antenna.
50 inches is still much too long for this frequency and fraction of a wavelength.
12 inches is too short for a quarter-wave at 146 MHz.

T9A09.hint: A 6-meter half-wave dipole is much longer than a 2-meter handheld antenna. Which approximate length fits that band?
T9A09.explanation: A half-wave 6 meter dipole is about 112 inches long. Since 6 meters is a much lower frequency than 2 meters, the antenna must be much longer.
T9A09.mnemonic: Six-meter half wave is roughly one hundred twelve inches.
why_wrong: 6 inches is nowhere near long enough for a half-wave antenna on 6 meters.
50 inches is still much too short for a half-wave 6-meter dipole.
236 inches is much too long for the approximate half-wave length being asked here.

T9A10.hint: A half-wave dipole radiates strongest off its sides, not off its tips. Which direction describes that?
T9A10.explanation: A half-wave dipole radiates the strongest signal broadside to the antenna. In other words, the strongest radiation is perpendicular to the wire, not off the ends.
T9A10.mnemonic: Dipoles are strongest off the sides.
why_wrong: A dipole is not equally strong in all directions.
Off the ends is where a dipole is weakest, not strongest.
The feed line direction is not what determines the main radiation lobe here.

T9A11.hint: Antenna gain does not create extra transmitter power. It means the antenna focuses radiation better in some directions than a reference antenna does.
T9A11.explanation: Antenna gain is the increase in signal strength in a specified direction compared to a reference antenna. The antenna redistributes energy rather than creating new power.
T9A11.mnemonic: Gain means focus compared with a reference.
why_wrong: An antenna does not add transmitter power on its own.
Higher-frequency operation does not mean the antenna suddenly requires added power in the way described.
Gain is not defined as a change in impedance.

T9A12.hint: A 5/8-wave mobile whip is popular because it can put a little more signal where you want it than a quarter-wave whip. What is its main advantage?
T9A12.explanation: A 5/8-wavelength whip has more gain than a 1/4-wavelength antenna. In practical terms, it can concentrate more energy toward lower radiation angles that are useful for many mobile contacts.
T9A12.mnemonic: Five-eighths wave gives a gain edge over quarter wave.
why_wrong: Very high radiation angles are not the reason this antenna is valued for mobile use.
It does not eliminate multipath distortion as a special feature.
The gain advantage is modest, not a ten-times power increase.

T9B01.hint: When SWR is low, more of your transmitter power gets into the antenna system instead of being reflected back. What benefit does that give?
T9B01.explanation: Low SWR means reduced signal loss because the antenna system is better matched to the feed line. A better match wastes less power in reflections and mismatch effects.
T9B01.mnemonic: Low SWR means less wasted signal.
why_wrong: Good SWR does not automatically solve TV interference problems.
Antenna wear is not the main concept measured by SWR.
Since the other choices are not generally true benefits of low SWR, 'all' is not correct.

T9B02.hint: Most amateur coax and radio gear are designed around one standard impedance. Which value is it?
T9B02.explanation: The most common impedance of coaxial cable used in amateur radio is 50 ohms. That is the standard most radios, antennas, connectors, and accessories are intended to work with.
why_wrong: 8 ohms is a speaker-related number, not the standard impedance for amateur coax feed lines.
600 ohms is associated with some balanced lines, not common coax in amateur stations.
12 ohms is not a standard amateur coax impedance.

T9B03.hint: Coax is popular not because it is perfect, but because it is practical and convenient in ordinary stations. Why is it so common?
T9B03.explanation: Coaxial cable is the most common amateur-radio feed line because it is easy to use and requires few special installation considerations. It is convenient to route, shielded, and works well in typical station setups.
T9B03.mnemonic: Coax wins because it is easy and practical.
why_wrong: Coax does not always have the lowest loss of any feed line type.
It is not true that coax handles more power than every other feed line type.
Coax is not guaranteed to be the least expensive option in every situation.

T9B04.hint: An antenna tuner does not magically fix the antenna itself. Its main job is to help the radio see the right impedance.
T9B04.explanation: The major function of an antenna tuner is to match the antenna system impedance to the transceiver's output impedance. That helps the transmitter work into a better load, even if the antenna system itself is not naturally resonant on that frequency.
why_wrong: A tuner is not an automatic weak-signal receiver aid.
An antenna can already be used for transmit and receive without that being the tuner's main purpose.
A tuner does not normally choose antennas for you.

T9B05.hint: As frequency goes up, coax generally gets more lossy, not less. What happens to feed-line loss as signal frequency increases?
T9B05.explanation: As the frequency of a signal in coaxial cable increases, the loss increases. This is why feed-line choice matters more and more at VHF, UHF, and above.
why_wrong: The question is about loss with frequency, not about characteristic impedance changing.
Coax loss does not decrease as frequency rises.
Again, characteristic impedance is not the effect being asked about here.

T9B06.hint: For frequencies above 400 MHz, you want a connector designed to behave well at UHF and microwave-ish ranges. Which one is the best choice listed?
T9B06.explanation: Type N connectors are most suitable for frequencies above 400 MHz because they maintain good RF performance at higher frequencies better than common UHF connectors.
T9B06.mnemonic: Need higher-frequency performance? Think Type N.
why_wrong: PL-259 and SO-239 UHF connectors are common, but they are not the best choice at higher frequencies.
RS-213 is not an RF connector type at all.
DB-25 is a multi-pin data connector, not a coax RF connector.

T9B07.hint: PL-259 connectors are the classic old-school ham coax connectors. Where are they commonly used?
T9B07.explanation: PL-259 type coax connectors are commonly used at HF and VHF frequencies. They are familiar and widespread in amateur stations, though not the best choice at higher UHF and microwave frequencies.
T9B07.mnemonic: PL-259 lives mostly in the HF and VHF world.
why_wrong: They are not preferred for microwave operation.
Standard PL-259 connectors are not inherently watertight.
A bayonet-type connector is more like a BNC, not a PL-259.

T9B08.hint: Coax loss can come from bad conditions, bad matching, and extra connection points. Which answer includes all those sources?
T9B08.explanation: Water intrusion into connectors, high SWR, and multiple connectors in the line can all contribute to feed-line loss. Because each listed item can add loss, 'All these choices are correct' is the right answer.
T9B08.mnemonic: Water, mismatch, and extra connectors all waste signal.
why_wrong: Water intrusion definitely causes loss, but it is only one of several causes listed.
High SWR can increase effective loss too, but it is not the only source named here.
Extra connectors add loss, but again this is only part of the full answer.

T9B09.hint: If SWR readings jump around unpredictably from one moment to the next, think intermittent hardware trouble rather than weather reports or modulation style.
T9B09.explanation: Erratic changes in SWR are often caused by a loose connection in the antenna or feed line. Intermittent physical connections make the impedance seen by the transmitter change unpredictably.
T9B09.mnemonic: Jumping SWR often means a loose connection.
why_wrong: A thunderstorm may create noise, but it is not the usual reason your SWR suddenly changes erratically.
Over-modulation affects audio quality, not feed-line SWR in this way.
Receiver overload from a strong station does not explain erratic SWR readings.

T9B10.hint: RG-213 is a larger, lower-loss coax than RG-58. What is the main electrical difference tested here?
T9B10.explanation: RG-213 has less loss than RG-58 at a given frequency. That is one reason operators use thicker coax when they want better performance, especially as frequency or run length increases.
why_wrong: There is a meaningful difference, especially in attenuation.
The question is not about shield count on RG-58.
RG-58 does not handle higher power better than RG-213 in the way suggested here.

T9B11.hint: If the question asks for the lowest-loss feed line at VHF and UHF, think of the big, rigid transmission line used on serious installations.
T9B11.explanation: Air-insulated hardline has the lowest loss of the listed feed lines at VHF and UHF. Its construction is optimized for very low attenuation, which is why it is used in demanding repeater and base-station installations.
T9B11.mnemonic: Lowest loss usually points to hardline.
why_wrong: Flexible 50-ohm coax is convenient, but not the lowest-loss option here.
That is not the kind of line normally chosen for minimum VHF/UHF loss.
Flexible 75-ohm coax is also not the lowest-loss choice in this comparison.

T9B12.hint: SWR tells you how well the feed line and load agree with each other electrically. What does it measure?
T9B12.explanation: Standing wave ratio is a measure of how well a load is matched to a transmission line. A lower SWR means a better match and less reflected power.
T9B12.mnemonic: SWR is the match meter for line and load.
why_wrong: SWR is not an amplifier power ratio.
It is not the general efficiency ratio of the transmitter itself.
It does not directly measure station grounding quality.
